EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rattlesnake Fire started on September 1 by unknown cause. It was located 2 miles West of Seven Bays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rattlesnake Fire started on September 1 by unknown cause. It was located 2 miles West of Seven Bays. It burned 21845 acres of brush and grass. It was contained on September 28. Cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved into the Pacific Northwest enhancing ongoing shower activity and lowering snow levels. This allowed for heavy snow to fall across the southwest Washington Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Surprise Lake SNOTEL at 4280 ft recorded 13 inches of snow between 1800 3/12 and 2000 3/13 with 12 inches hit 1930 3/13, and the Swift Creek SNOTEL at 4440 ft recorded 12 inches of snow between 1800 3/12 and 1900 3/13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level shortwave trough and accompanying occluded front followed by a shower post-frontal airmass facilitated heavy snowfall across the southwest Washington Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Swift Creek SNOTEL at 4440 ft recorded 19 inches of snowfall between 1700 3/19 and 2100 3/20 with 12 inches reached 1300 3/20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad, parent low centered off the BC coast circulated a series of shortwaves into the Pacific Northwest from February 15th to the 18th, leading to persistent mountain snow over the period. This was an extension of the previous Winter Storm Warning initially issued on Feb 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Touchet Snotel recorded a storm total snow of 15 inches early the morning of the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and moist westerly flow aloft January 31st into February 1st resulted in enhanced orographic lift into the Northern Blue Mountains,  with heavy snow accumulations occurring across portions of these mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Touchet Snotel site measured 11 inches of new snow in a 26 hour period ending at 0600 Feb. 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system, a weak to moderate atmospheric river, and the development of a stationary front across the area resulted in widespread moderate to heavy snow across the mountains and lower elevations of eastern Oregon and southeastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cocorahs observer WA-CB-3 9 miles northeast Dayton reported 5 inches of new snow at 0700 February 5th. The Cocorahs observer WA-CB-2 3 miles south southeast of Dayton measured 4.5 inches of new snow at 0700 February 5th. Radar and observation estimated ending of snowfall occurred before 0430 on February 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two quick hitting shortwave troughs brought widespread and sustained precipitation to the western Washington allowing for heavy snowfall across the Cascades which was bolstered by a third deep upper trough towards the end of the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Swift Creek SNOTEL at 4440 ft recorded 21 inches of snowfall between 1500 3/14 and 2200 03/15 with 12 inches reached 0400 3/15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system, a weak to moderate atmospheric river, and the development of a stationary front across the area resulted in widespread moderate to heavy snow across the mountains and lower elevations of eastern Oregon and southeastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cocorahs observer WA-KL-6 1 mile west northwest of White Salmon reported 3.8 inches of new snow in a 48 hour period ending 0700 February 6th. Radar estimated end of snowfall occurred around 0100 February 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front sweeping through the Pacific Northwest led to strong wind gusts across the Columbia Basin during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 23rd. The strongest wind gusts were recorded in the lower Columbia Basin of Washington in particular.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sites a part of the Hanford Site Mesonet (Gable West and the Emergency Operations Center) recorded max wind gusts of 60 and 59 mph, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low brought widespread high winds to the forecast area during the day on Feb 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer in Finley reported a 69 mph gust, and several stations part of the Hanford Site mesonet reported wind gusts of at least 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SLOPES OF THE EST WASHINGTON CASCADE CREST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough and surface cold front passage enhanced by an atmospheric river produced bands of moderate to heavy snowfall across portions of our mountain zones within the atmospheric river path through the weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bumping Ridge SNOTEL measured 13 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 1900 PDT on March 15th. The White Pass SNOTEL measured 12 inches of new snow in a 31 hour period ending at 0000 PDT on March 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough and surface cold front passage enhanced by an atmospheric river produced bands of moderate to heavy snowfall across portions of our mountain zones within the atmospheric river path through the weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Touchet SNOTEL measured 10 inches of new snow in a 12 hour period ending at 1600 PDT on March 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Katy Creek Wildfire started on August 30 by lightning. It was located 6 miles Northwest of Kettle Falls, Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Katy Creek Wildfire started on August 30 by lightning. It was located 6 miles Northwest of Kettle Falls, Washington. It burned 4680 acres of timber. It was contained on September 28. Cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Crown Creek Wildfire started on August 29 by lightning. It was located 1 mile Southeast of Marble, Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crown Creek Wildfire started on August 29 by lightning. It was located 1 mile Southeast of Marble, Washington. One home was reported lost. It burned 14192 acres of timber. It was contained on September 14. Cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud reported 10 miles Northwest of Soap Lake on September 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported a funnel cloud near Soap Lake. No damage was reported. Location estimated from radar data and photo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Labor Mountain Fire started on September 1 by lightning. It was located 10 miles North of Cle Elum.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Labor Mountain Fire started on September 1 by lightning. It was located 10 miles North of Cle Elum. It burned 42721 acres of timber. It continued through September. Cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded front coupled with a moist southwest flow aloft lead to heavy snow developing across portions of eastern and central Oregon as well as across portions of south central Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cocorahs observer WA-KL-6 1 mile west northwest of White Salmon reported 5 inches of new snow ending at 0700 on February 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad, parent low centered off the BC coast circulated a series of shortwaves into the Pacific Northwest from February 15th to the 18th, leading to persistent mountain snow over the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ski Bluewood reported 11 inches of snow in 12 hours from 4am to 4pm Feb 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river moved through the region on February 22-26. Many locations across Eastern Washington received rain amounts 1 to 2 inches over the period. The rain combined with snow melt in the area leading to several reports of flooding from Pullman to Spokane. Little Spokane River at Dartford crested at 5.82 feet. The Latah Creek at Spokane crested at 12.82 feet. The South Fork of the Palouse River at Pullman crested 7.64 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding in Department of Highways facility with approximately 2 to 3 inches of water in shop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Arctic front pushed through the region with ample moisture. Temperatures were sub freezing across the region. It brought widespread snow showers to the eastern Washington. Mountain peaks ranged from 8 to 12 inches of snow. Most low land areas received 3 to 7 inches. An observer near Anatone reported 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported an estimated 5 inches of snow fell 7 miles northwest of Mallot.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific frontal system swept across Western Washington resulting in heavy snowfall totals in the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixteen inches of snowfall was reported at Paradise at Mount Rainier National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally cold conditions were in place early on February 13th as widespread precipitation increased across the region south to north due to the arrival of a warm-frontal boundary. This overrunning scenario led to a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain impacting much of western Oregon through February 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHs station 1 miles WNW of La center measured 3.9 inches of snowfall at 0800 2/14 with local storm reports confirming around 2-3.5 inches of total snowfall through the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally cold conditions were in place early on February 13th as widespread precipitation increased across the region south to north due to the arrival of a warm-frontal boundary. This overrunning scenario led to a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain impacting much of western Oregon through February 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public measured 3.5 inches of snowfall at 0830 2/14 in downtown Vancouver, WA, and local storm reports showed a consistent 2.5-3.5 inches of snowfall across the forecast zone from event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally cold conditions were in place early on February 13th as widespread precipitation increased across the region south to north due to the arrival of a warm-frontal boundary. This overrunning scenario led to a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain impacting much of western Oregon through February 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public measured 3 inches of snowfall in Washougal at 0830 2/14, which is a warning level amount.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally cold conditions were in place early on February 13th as widespread precipitation increased across the region south to north due to the arrival of a warm-frontal boundary. This overrunning scenario led to a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain impacting much of western Oregon through February 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 3.5 inches of snow at 1800 2/13 with local storm reports indicating 2-3.5 inches of snowfall across the forecast zone. Additionally, due to the snow there was a 22 vehicle collision, involving multiple commercial vehicles & passenger cars in Lewis & Cowlitz Counties. 6 people were transported to 3 area hospitals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally cold conditions were in place early on February 13th as widespread precipitation increased across the region south to north due to the arrival of a warm-frontal boundary. This overrunning scenario led to a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain impacting much of western Oregon through February 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on local storm reports in the region, there is high confidence 2-4.5 inches of snowfall accumulated by the morning of 2/14, thus verifying the warning. WSDOT also required chains and/or traction tires 02/13 to 02/14 due to the deteriorated road conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The followed Local Storm Reports were observed near the boarder of the forecast zone at a lower elevation: 6 in snow at Ilwaco 2/4 10 am, 6 in snow at Naselle 2/4 0830 AM, LSR, and 5 in snow near Oceanside 2/4 12pm. These combined with the Saddle Mountain SNOTEL at 3090ft recording 6 inches of snowfall between 0500 02/05 and 1700 02/05 would indicate snowfall meeting warning criteria very likely occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally cold conditions were in place early on February 13th as widespread precipitation increased across the region south to north due to the arrival of a warm-frontal boundary. This overrunning scenario led to a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain impacting much of western Oregon through February 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on local storm reports in the region, there is high confidence 2-4.5 inches of snowfall accumulated by the morning of 2/14, thus verifying the warning.  WSDOT also required chains and/or traction tires on SR-14 02/13 to 02/14 due to the deteriorated road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening upper-level low and an associated strong low pressure system at the surface tracked just off the Pacific Northwest coast leading to a period of high winds. Heavy snow also fell across the south Washington Cascades during this period in addition to localized flooding on the Grays River near the coast. The frontal passage during the early afternoon hours of Feb 24th allowed for a period of isolated severe thunderstorms later that afternoon due to a low CAPE but high shear environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station 82sw1 Cape Disappointment: Peak gust 85 mph 02/24 2200 PST, near or above 58 mph 1900 02/24 to 0400 02/25. In addition, the mesonet station ODT 76 Megler Bridge; peak gust 76 mph 02/25 0106 PST, near or above 58mph 02/24 1300 to 02/25 0400. Also, the Holy Cross Radio communications tower which provides the region with its primary cell coverage broke in half from wind between midnight and 1 am. The tower is located on atop a hill outside of Raymond above the rock quarry.  This affected several thousand cell phone users.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river moved through the region on February 22-26. Many locations across Eastern Washington received rain amounts 1 to 2 inches over the period. The rain combined with snow melt in the area leading to several reports of flooding from Pullman to Spokane. Little Spokane River at Dartford crested at 5.82 feet. The Latah Creek at Spokane crested at 12.82 feet. The South Fork of the Palouse River at Pullman crested 7.64 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred in town of Garfield with water up to homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dry cold front moved through Eastern Washington on May 31. Wind speeds across the Basin ranged from the upper 30 mph range to low 50 mph range. Highest wind gust of 55 mph was reported by a RAWS station in Douglas. Winds over Northeast Washington ranged from 20 to 40 mph. These winds were strong enough to knock down trees over some of the highways in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington DOT reported trees down along SR 21 near mile marker 187.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The dry cold front moved through the region during the afternoon and early evening hours. Wind speeds were in the teens with gusts up to 30 MPH being reported. The Basin was experiencing a prolonged period of lack of precip. It led to blowing dust reducing visibility and an auto accident along interstate 90. It also caused tree damage and power outages for portions of Northeast Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northbound lane of SR25 blocked due to downed trees. An agrimet site on Lions Island near Kettle Falls recorded a gust of 39 MPH at 1425 LST. Utility companies reported 2100 customers lost power in Stevens county near Fruitland and 1728 customers lost power in Pend Oreille County near Usk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds on August 7th contributed to a localized dust storm along HWY 395 in central Washington, near the Franklin-Adams County line, attributed primarily to a tilled, unirrigated field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convergence zone stretched across the Eastern Washington bringing decent snow to the east slopes of the Cascades. Blewett Pass received 9 inches of snow and had travel issues with the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer measured 9 inches of at Blewett Pass. The Pass was closed at milepost 164 from 0630-1000 on October 13. Reports of disabled semis and collisions from the Department of Transportation. Damage cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A gust front pushed through Eastern Washington during the afternoon of October 25. Wind gusts generally ranged from 30 to 40 mph. Localized areas in Spokane and Lewiston areas saw gusts near 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely localized swath of wind speeds estimated at 55-68 mph resulted in several trees uprooted/blown down in Audubon neighborhood including Audubon Elem, through intersection of Chelan Ave/Ash Street with minor damage noted. Spokane Airport recorded a gusts of 49 mph at 1535 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lynx Mountain Fire started on September 2 by suspected human cause. It was located 7 miles Northwest of Inchelium. It was contained late October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lynx Mountain Fire started on September 2 by suspected human cause. It was located 7 miles Northwest of Inchelium. It burned 9226 acres of brush and timber. Cost is estimated. Containment date is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low quickly deepened offshore due west of the mouth of the Columbia River and progressed towards the Olympic Peninsula allowing for a tight surface pressure gradient to drive strong to locally high winds region-wide. This surface gradient was also aided by a strong band of west-southwest winds just above the surface (often called a sting jet) which pivoted over the region through the day on Oct. 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 59 mph was measured at the Toke Point mesonet station at 1930 PST, and this station was at or near warning criteria from 1700 PST to 2000 PST. Additional a 60mph peak gust was measured at the Astoria-Megler Bridge mesonet station at 1743 PST. This station was at or near warning criteria 1300 to 1930 PST. It's worth noting the Pacific County Emergency Manager estimated around $60,000 dollars worth of damage was produced from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A board upper-level and secondary trough feature dropping southward from Canada in addition to cold air aloft supported convective activity. Some of this strong shower and thunderstorm activity was was unusually impactful producing locally severe outflow winds and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a funnel cloud associated with a strong rain shower around 3:53 PM PDT Oct. 12, 2025 in La Center, Washington. There were no reports of a tornado on the ground and/or wind damage with this cell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A board upper-level and secondary trough feature dropping southward from Canada in addition to cold air aloft supported convective activity. Some of this strong shower and thunderstorm activity was was unusually impactful producing locally severe outflow winds and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS Damage Assessment Team surveyed wind damage from a strong convective shower that moved through Longview, Washington around 3:13 PM PDT Oct. 12, 2025. Based on the wind damage, estimated peak winds of 80-85 mph occurred, most likely with a wet microburst that produced damaging straight-line winds over a small geographic area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy rains to the Cascades December 8 through 10. The East slopes saw 48 hour rain amounts of up to 7 inches along the crest, 3 to 4 inches in the mid slopes, and at least half an inch in the low land areas. The rain combined with snow melt and led to several flooding and landslide incidents. The flooding forced the closure of highway US 2 for several weeks. An embedded cold front brought high winds in the 60 mph range to the region causing downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station DVI09189 located 3 miles East of Dodge recorded a gust of 57 mph at 1045 LST.

Blewett Pass was closed from 1800 Dec 10 to 1500 Dec 11 due to trees across US 97. Damage is estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of flood impacts  were received from the Lake Chelan Recreation Area and Stehekin. A rockslide took place outside the Pioneer burn area from 2024 and blocked the road, damaging 200 yards of the road between Purple Point and Loggers Point. Several debris flows coming down onto roads in the area, including Stehekin Valley Road. The town of Stehekin experienced a debris flow from the Pioneer Fire Burn Scar. A debris flow has washed out multiple roads, and swept away the water treatment plant and sewer system. They are currently receiving pallets of water and other resources. Damage cost is estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of flooding impacts were received from the Lake Wenatchee and Coles Corner area. Reports of homes being flooded on Cedar Brae Road along Lake Wenatchee with water rescues being needed. US 2 was closed from Coles Corner at MP 85 through Tumwater Canyon to Leavenworth at MP 99 due to high water. A landslide occurred on southbound SR 207 near Coles Corner, launching a large tree through the windshield of a truck and injuring the driver. US 2 is closed from milepost 57 at Scenic to milepost 85 at Coles Corner including Stevens Pass Summit due to rocks, trees, and mud over the roadway in multiple locations. White River Road and Little Wenatchee River Road at Lake Wenatchee closed because of water over the roadway. Damage cost is estimated.

Mile marker 97 US Hwy 2 Tumwater canyon road washed out from high river water eroding away the highway. Road closed December 11 through December 15. Waterfront and Enchantment Parks are both under water due to the rising river level. This includes Blackbird Island. Emergency Manager reported water over road on Chumstick Highway near MP3. Damage cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system supported by a negatively tilted upper-level trough drove a cold-frontal boundary through the region supporting a period of high winds. This system coinciding with the arrival of Perigean Spring Tides (also known as King Tides) also led to localized coastal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video posted on social media showed water across the road in Raymond Washington near the intersection of Willapa Pl Way and Ocean Ave at 1315 on Thursday November 6th. It appears a car was stuck in the water and was in the process of getting towed/rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system supported by a negatively tilted upper-level trough drove a cold-frontal boundary through the region supporting a period of high winds. This system coinciding with the arrival of Perigean Spring Tides (also known as King Tides) also led to localized coastal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph peak wind gust measured twice at HKFW1 (Huckleberry RAWS) at 0211 and again 0511 on 11/5 with wind gusts near to above warning level criteria 0200 11/5 to 0600 11/5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and high pressure overhead allowed for radiative cooling processes to take over and cool the eastern Columbia River Gorge to below freezing across much of the zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various mesonet and Oregon Department of Transportation stations as well as the ASOS at the Dalles Municipal Airport reported sub-freezing temperatures for several hours between 2am and 8am.
